dimensions.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether the person processes advertising primarily through emotional/visceral reactions (mood, music, visuals, storytelling) or through analytical evaluation (claims, comparisons, evidence). This is distinct from what content they prefer (Q59 covers content type preference) — this is about the cognitive mode they default to when an ad interrupts their feed. Do they feel first and rationalize later, or do they evaluate first and feel later?
dimensions.concept_test_impact: An emotional processor would respond strongly to a concept framed around transformation narratives ('imagine stepping out feeling unstoppable') while an analytical processor with the same profile would dismiss that as fluff and respond better to a concept structured as problem → ingredient → mechanism → result. When shown a concept card, the emotional processor evaluates 'does this excite me?' while the analytical one evaluates 'does this make sense?'
dimensions.ad_test_impact: A video ad with cinematic visuals, evocative music, and minimal text would generate high engagement from an emotional processor but low purchase intent from an analytical one. Conversely, a straightforward ad with ingredient callouts and clinical data would feel compelling to the analytical processor but boring/skippable to the emotional one. This determines whether the first 3 seconds hook them or lose them.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: His preference for 'ingredient breakdown' and 'aesthetic content' (Q59) and his trust in expert opinion (Q11) suggest analytical leanings. But his Bath & Body Works purchase was triggered by the 'aesthetic mall store experience' (Q23) — a clearly emotional/sensory trigger. His doom-scrolling behavior (Q56) suggests passive consumption where emotional hooks may dominate. The tension between these signals makes his default processing mode genuinely uncertain.
dimensions.example_variation: Person A (emotional processor) sees a body wash ad with a slow-motion shower scene, warm lighting, and a tagline 'Your skin deserves this moment' — feels drawn in, saves the post, considers buying. Person B (analytical processor) with identical 59 answers sees the same ad, finds it generic, scrolls past, and would only stop if the ad opened with 'Sulphate-free formula clinically tested on Indian skin types.' Same product, vastly different engagement.

dimensions.dimension_name: Social Identity Signaling Through Product Choices
dimensions.what_it_captures: The degree to which this person views body wash as a social identity marker — something that signals who they are to peers, romantic interests, or social circles — versus a purely functional/private product. This goes beyond Q8's 'smelling good for others' to capture whether the brand itself, its packaging visible in their bathroom, or being able to mention it in conversation matters to their self-concept and social positioning.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A high social-signaling person would respond more favorably to a concept positioned as 'the body wash discerning men choose' with premium-looking packaging designed to be noticed, while a low social-signaling person would prefer a concept emphasizing efficacy and value regardless of how it looks on a shelf or in their bathroom. Premium pricing would be justified differently — one pays for status, the other for performance.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: An ad featuring aspirational lifestyle cues (upscale apartment, well-dressed man, subtle luxury markers) would drive purchase intent for a high social-signaler but feel irrelevant or even off-putting to someone who sees body wash as deeply private. Influencer content would also land differently — the social-signaler cares about the influencer's aspirational status, while the non-signaler only cares about the influencer's expertise.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: He was drawn to Bath & Body Works partly for the premium store experience (Q23), suggesting some social-signaling tendency. He prefers minimalist packaging (Q41) which could signal understated sophistication or genuine indifference to aesthetics. His household income (>₹2L) enables premium choices, but he found the ₹1000 Bath & Body Works 'not worth it' (Q51). Whether that rejection was about performance failing or social value failing is unclear. His self-description as 'smart' and 'passionate' (Q6) doesn't clarify whether products are identity extensions.
dimensions.example_variation: Both see a concept for a ₹450 body wash with sleek, Instagram-worthy packaging and the tagline 'Elevated daily ritual.' Person A (high social signaling) imagines it in their bathroom, thinks about how it reflects their taste, and rates purchase intent highly. Person B (low social signaling) ignores the packaging narrative entirely, flips to the ingredient list, and rates purchase intent based solely on whether the formulation addresses oiliness and acne.

dimensions.dimension_name: Receptivity to Masculinity Framing in Marketing
dimensions.what_it_captures: How the person responds to gendered marketing cues — specifically whether masculine-coded messaging (rugged imagery, 'for men' labels, deep/woody/intense descriptors, male-only models) increases or decreases their engagement. Some young men in metro India embrace traditionally masculine branding; others find it regressive, performative, or limiting and prefer gender-neutral positioning. This isn't about fragrance preference (covered in Q35-Q36) but about the entire gendered framing of the product and its communication.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A concept positioned as 'engineered for men's skin' with dark packaging and bold typography would strongly appeal to someone high on masculinity-framing receptivity but could feel cliché or exclusionary to someone who prefers gender-neutral brands. The same formulation positioned as 'for all skin types' with neutral design would reverse these reactions. This affects perceived relevance, willingness to try, and price sensitivity.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: An ad featuring hyper-masculine cues (gym scenes, deep voiceover, 'man up your shower') would generate strong positive or negative reactions depending on this dimension. Someone resistant to masculinity framing might engage more with an ad showing diverse users or focusing purely on skin science without gendered language. This dimension determines whether 'The Man Company' style branding is a draw or a deterrent.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: He described his ideal body wash as having a 'masculine aftershave-like fragrance' (Q49), suggesting some comfort with masculine framing. He follows 'male actors and male beauty influencers' (Q55) and rated The Man Company 4/5 and Beardo 2/5 (Q48) — but we don't know if those ratings reflect gendered branding appeal or product quality. He also rated Plum, Dot & Key, and Minimalist (all gender-neutral brands) at 5/5. Whether he wants masculine positioning or just happened to describe a fragrance preference remains ambiguous.
dimensions.example_variation: Both see an ad for a new body wash: muscular male model, dark bottle, tagline 'Built for men who mean business.' Person A (high masculinity-framing receptivity) feels the ad speaks directly to them, perceives the product as more effective for their needs, and clicks through. Person B (low receptivity) finds the ad try-hard and cringe, associates it with outdated marketing, and scrolls past — even though the underlying product formulation would interest them.

dimensions.dimension_name: Decision Urgency and Deliberation Window Length
dimensions.what_it_captures: The time span between first exposure to a product concept/ad and the point at which a purchase decision is made — and how much active deliberation occurs in that window. This captures whether the person is an impulse-to-quick converter (sees ad → buys within hours/days) or a slow-burn evaluator (sees ad → researches over weeks → waits for a trigger like running out). This is distinct from purchase channel (Q20-Q21) and discovery source (Q54) — it's about the temporal and cognitive architecture of the decision itself.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A quick converter would respond to concept tests with more decisive purchase intent scores — they simulate the decision as if buying now. A slow-burn evaluator would give more hedged responses ('I'd look into it') and their stated purchase intent would underpredict actual conversion because they need multiple exposures and a situational trigger. Concepts with urgency cues (limited edition, introductory pricing) would disproportionately move quick converters.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: A single-exposure ad test would overestimate impact on quick converters and underestimate impact on slow-burn evaluators. For the slow-burn type, retargeting sequences and multi-touchpoint campaigns would be necessary. An ad with a strong CTA ('Order now on Blinkit') would convert the quick type but feel pushy to the deliberate type. This dimension determines whether a single ad impression or a sustained campaign drives action.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: His Plum journey (Q58) shows a multi-stage process: social media awareness → seeing on Blinkit → further research → trial. This suggests slow-burn evaluation. But his Blinkit usage (Q25) shows impulsive, need-triggered purchasing ('realizes product is empty right before bathing'). These seem contradictory — he may be slow-burn for new brands but impulsive for repurchases. His deliberation window for genuinely new concepts remains unclear, and this matters enormously for predicting concept test responses.
dimensions.example_variation: Both see the same body wash concept in an Instagram ad with a 'Shop Now' button. Person A (short deliberation window) taps through, reads the product page, and adds to cart within 10 minutes — their concept test response would show high purchase intent. Person B (long deliberation window) screenshots the ad, plans to research later, and may or may not follow through — their concept test response would show moderate interest but low immediate intent, even though they might eventually buy at the same rate.

dimensions.dimension_name: Skepticism Toward Novel Product Claims and Positioning
dimensions.what_it_captures: The person's baseline skepticism when encountering unfamiliar or ambitious product claims — particularly claims that combine multiple benefits, use scientific-sounding language, or promise differentiation from existing products. This is different from trust in information sources (Q18) and different from what claims attract attention (Q50). This captures whether, once a claim has attracted their attention, they default to believing it or default to doubting it and requiring proof. It's the 'prove it to me' threshold.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A highly skeptical person would discount bold concept claims ('eliminates body odor for 48 hours,' 'replaces your moisturizer') and rate purchase intent lower unless substantiation is provided within the concept itself. A less skeptical person would take claims at face value and rate intent higher. This means the same concept would need different levels of evidence density to convert different people. Multi-benefit concepts (like the 2-in-1 or 3-in-1 he encountered) would be particularly polarizing.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: An ad making aspirational claims without evidence ('transforms your skin in 7 days') would generate excitement in a low-skepticism person but trigger active resistance in a high-skepticism person. The high-skepticism person would respond better to ads that preemptively address doubt — showing clinical trial results, dermatologist endorsements with specific credentials, or before/after evidence. The format of substantiation (data vs. testimonial vs. demonstration) that satisfies their skepticism is also variable.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: He was skeptical of Bath & Body Works' 3-in-1 claim and validated it through personal experience — finding it 'subpar' (Q23, Q51). He values clinical studies (Q50) and expert opinion (Q11), suggesting a higher skepticism threshold. He used ChatGPT to verify dermatologist routines (Q34), showing independent verification behavior. However, he rated Plum, Minimalist, and Dot & Key all at 5/5 (Q48) — brands that rely heavily on marketing claims. We don't know if he verified those claims or accepted them based on brand aesthetics and social proof. His skepticism threshold for different claim types (efficacy vs. ingredient purity vs. multi-benefit) remains unmeasured.
dimensions.example_variation: Both see a concept for a body wash claiming 'Clinically proven to reduce dark spots in 4 weeks while controlling oil for 12 hours — powered by 2% niacinamide and volcanic ash.' Person A (high skepticism) thinks 'that's too many claims, probably none work well,' rates believability low, and would only consider buying after finding independent reviews or clinical data. Person B (low skepticism) thinks 'niacinamide is legit, volcanic ash sounds innovative,' takes the clinical claim at face value, and rates high purchase intent immediately.

archetypes.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style

selected_question.question_text: You're scrolling through Instagram and a body wash ad stops you mid-scroll. Think about the last time that actually happened — what was it about the ad that made you pause? Was it something you saw or felt (like a vibe, aesthetic, or mood), or something you read or heard (like a claim, ingredient, or fact)? Walk me through what grabbed you and what happened next in your head.
selected_question.why_selected: This question directly probes the cognitive mode this person defaults to when an ad interrupts passive scrolling — the exact moment the dimension matters most. It's grounded in his real behavior (doom-scrolling on Instagram per Q56, discovering Plum via social media per Q58, and buying from quick commerce after seeing things on Instagram per Q25). The open-ended 'walk me through' format lets him reveal whether his first reaction is visceral/aesthetic or evaluative/analytical, without leading him toward either. None of the 291 bank questions capture this — they focus on purchase mechanics or information-seeking order, not the cognitive processing mode during ad exposure. Q40 from the bank comes closest but asks about research sequencing, not the initial hook moment. This generated question isolates the first 2-3 seconds of ad processing that determines engagement vs. scroll-past.

archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, it's usually the vibe that gets me. Like if the lighting is warm, the bottle looks clean and premium, and the whole thing just feels aspirational — I'll stop. It's like that Bath & Body Works store experience but on my phone. I might notice the aesthetic first and then check if the ingredients make sense later, but the mood of the ad is what hooks me. If it looks like a Nautica ad or something with that ocean-fresh energy, I'm pausing for sure.
archetypes.why_plausible: His Bath & Body Works purchase was triggered by the 'aesthetic mall store experience' (Q23) — a purely sensory/emotional trigger. He values 'aesthetic content' as a top content type (Q59). His ideal body wash includes a 'masculine aftershave-like fragrance' (Q49) suggesting he thinks in sensory/aspirational terms. His doom-scrolling behavior (Q56) means he's in a passive, emotionally receptive state when encountering ads.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would respond strongly to concept cards with premium visual design, lifestyle imagery, and transformation narratives ('step out feeling unstoppable'). Would rate concepts higher when packaging mockups look Instagram-worthy and the brand story evokes aspiration. Would be less engaged by text-heavy concept cards even if the formulation is superior. A concept for his ideal body wash (Nautica scent + Dove texture) would score highest when presented with mood boards rather than ingredient tables.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would stop scrolling for cinematic body wash ads with slow-motion shower scenes, warm lighting, and evocative music. A 15-second Instagram Reel with premium aesthetics and minimal text would generate high save/share rates. Would skip ads that open with ingredient callouts or clinical data. Influencer ads would work best when the influencer looks aspirational and the content feels lifestyle-oriented rather than educational. Would recall the ad's mood and visual tone more than specific claims.

archetypes.answer_text: I think what actually stops me is when I see a specific claim that makes me go 'wait, is that real?' Like if a body wash says it's sulphate-free and clinically tested for oily skin in Indian conditions — that's interesting. I'll pause and check the ingredient list or see if they cite any study. The pretty visuals are everywhere, everyone does that. But if the ad actually tells me something useful about what's in it and why it works, that's when I engage. I'll probably screenshot it and look it up on Blinkit later.
archetypes.why_plausible: His top purchase factor is expert opinion, followed by ingredients (Q11). He trusts dermatologists most (5/5) and packaging claims least (1/5) (Q18). He's willing to pay premium only for clinical-grade formulations (Q16). He values 'ingredient breakdown' as top content type (Q59). He researched Plum further after seeing it on social media before buying (Q58). He used ChatGPT to find a simplified skincare routine based on evidence (Q34). His frustration with Bath & Body Works was that claims 'didn't hold up' (Q23).
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would respond best to concept cards structured as problem → ingredient → mechanism → result. Would scrutinize ingredient lists and dismiss vague claims like 'nourishing' or 'luxurious feel.' Would rate concepts higher when they include clinical study references, sulphate-free certification, or dermatologist endorsement. A concept for Indian-specific formulation (Q52) would need to explain exactly how it addresses oily skin and pollution — not just claim it. Would penalize concepts with beautiful packaging but weak ingredient rationale.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would scroll past cinematic/mood-based ads as generic. Would stop for ads that open with a specific claim or ingredient callout in the first 2-3 seconds (e.g., 'Niacinamide + Salicylic Acid for oily Indian skin'). Influencer ads would only work if the influencer does an actual ingredient breakdown or comparison. Would respond well to split-screen before/after formats or 'what's actually in your body wash' educational content. Would click through to product pages to verify claims before purchasing.

archetypes.answer_text: It's a two-step thing for me. The visual has to be clean and appealing enough to make me stop — like minimalist packaging, good color palette, not too cluttered. But once I've stopped, I'm immediately reading what's in it. If the ad just looks good but says nothing real, I'll scroll past in 3 seconds. And if it's ugly but has great ingredients, I probably never stopped to notice. So I need both — the aesthetic pulls me in, but the ingredients and claims are what make me actually consider buying it. That's basically what happened with Plum.
archetypes.why_plausible: This perfectly mirrors his Plum journey (Q58): discovered through heavy social media campaigns (aesthetic/emotional hook), didn't buy immediately, then researched further on Blinkit (analytical evaluation). His top 2 content types are 'ingredient breakdown' AND 'aesthetic content' (Q59) — he chose both, not one. His Bath & Body Works experience shows aesthetic triggers work on him (Q23), but his post-purchase dissatisfaction was analytical ('claims didn't hold up'). He wants minimalist packaging (Q41) but also values clinical-grade formulation (Q16). His ideal body wash combines sensory elements (aftershave fragrance, creamy texture) with functional requirements (sulphate-free, targets dark spots) (Q49).
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would engage with concepts that lead with clean, premium visual design but immediately back it up with ingredient logic. A concept card with a beautiful minimalist mockup AND a clear problem→solution ingredient story would score highest. Would reject concepts that are all aesthetic (no substance) or all clinical (no appeal). Would be the hardest archetype to satisfy but also the most loyal if a concept nails both. For Indian-specific formulation concepts, would need both an aspirational positioning and a credible mechanism.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: The first 2-3 seconds need aesthetic quality to prevent scrolling — clean visuals, good color grading, premium feel. But seconds 3-8 must deliver substance: ingredient callouts, a specific claim, or a credible mechanism. A two-phase ad structure (hook with mood → deliver with evidence) would maximize engagement. Influencer content works best when the influencer has good skin/aesthetic credibility AND does ingredient analysis. Would save ads that combine both elements and revisit them before purchasing on Blinkit. Would be frustrated by ads that are beautiful but empty, and bored by ads that are informative but visually generic.

archetypes.dimension_name: Social Identity Signaling Through Product Choices

selected_question.question_text: Two body washes have identical reviews and ingredients. One costs ₹300 with plain packaging, the other ₹450 with Instagram-worthy design. Which influences your purchase decision more?
selected_question.why_selected: This question isolates the social-signaling dimension perfectly for this person. Given his profile — he values ingredients highly (Q11, Q24), prefers minimalist packaging (Q41), browses quick commerce (Q25), and is active on Instagram (Q54) — this question creates a direct tension between his stated functional priorities and any latent desire for social identity expression through product aesthetics. The identical ingredients remove his primary decision lever (efficacy/formulation), forcing him to reveal whether the visual/social presentation of a product carries independent value. His Bath & Body Works experience (Q23) showed he was drawn to premium aesthetics but rejected on performance grounds — this question clarifies whether aesthetics alone hold weight when performance is equalized. The ₹150 premium is within his stated range (₹350-500, Q21) so price isn't a disqualifier, making the choice purely about social signaling vs. functional pragmatism.

archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, if the ingredients are literally the same, I'd go for the ₹450 one with the better design. I like having good-looking stuff in my bathroom — when friends come over or I'm posting a shelfie, it just reflects a certain taste. It's like how I went to Bath & Body Works partly because the store and packaging felt premium. ₹150 extra isn't much for something that looks and feels elevated.
archetypes.why_plausible: He was drawn to the Bath & Body Works store experience and premium feel (Q23), describes himself as 'smart' and 'passionate' (Q6), has high household income (>₹2L), and discovers products via Instagram (Q54). His preference for minimalist packaging (Q41) could reflect curated, understated sophistication rather than indifference — a form of deliberate taste signaling. He already spends ₹350-500 on body wash (Q21), so the ₹450 price point is comfortable.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would respond strongly to a body wash positioned as 'the discerning choice' with sleek, Instagram-worthy minimalist packaging. Would rate concepts higher when they include lifestyle imagery showing the product in an aspirational bathroom setting. Premium pricing justified by design and brand story would feel natural. Would be drawn to limited-edition or collab packaging.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage more with ads featuring aspirational lifestyle cues — well-designed apartments, curated routines, subtle luxury markers. Influencer content would resonate when the influencer has an aspirational aesthetic and lifestyle, not just skincare expertise. Social proof elements like 'trending among [desirable peer group]' would boost purchase intent. Would share or save aesthetically pleasing ad content.

archetypes.answer_text: ₹300, easy. If the ingredients and reviews are the same, why would I pay ₹150 more for packaging I'm going to throw away? I order on Blinkit anyway — I barely even see the packaging before it's in my bathroom. I'd rather save that ₹150 or put it toward a better face cleanser. The product inside the bottle is what matters to me.
archetypes.why_plausible: He ranks ingredients first and packaging last in purchase factors (Q11). He found Bath & Body Works 'not worth the premium' (Q51) and would look for alternatives if prices rose 30% (Q26). He buys primarily via quick commerce where packaging visibility is minimal (Q25). He pays 0% premium for sustainable packaging and only pays extra for clinical-grade formulation (Q16). His frustration with Dettol is about scent and skin feel, not aesthetics (Q45).
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would ignore packaging design and lifestyle positioning entirely. Would flip straight to ingredient list, clinical claims, and skin concern targeting (oiliness, acne, dark spots per Q49). Would rate a plain-packaged concept with strong formulation claims higher than a beautifully designed concept with vague benefits. Premium pricing only justified by demonstrable ingredient superiority.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would skip past lifestyle-heavy ads and engage only with ingredient breakdowns, dermatologist endorsements, or before/after evidence. Influencer content would only land if the influencer demonstrates product knowledge and has visible skin results — their lifestyle or status is irrelevant. Would find aspirational apartment/luxury ads disconnected and possibly inauthentic. Would respond to educational, science-forward content.

archetypes.answer_text: It depends on the design honestly. If the ₹450 one has that clean, minimalist look I like — not flashy but thoughtfully designed — I might pick it up because I appreciate good design. But I wouldn't pay extra just for something that's trying too hard to look premium. If the plain one looks decent enough, I'd save the money. I care about how things look in my space but I'm not paying a premium purely for aesthetics when I know the product inside is the same.
archetypes.why_plausible: He explicitly prefers minimalist packaging (Q41) but ranks packaging last in purchase factors (Q11) — suggesting he cares about design quality but won't pay a premium for it. His Bath & Body Works experience shows he's drawn to premium aesthetics but ultimately judges on performance (Q23, Q51). He values small-scale brands partly for their innovative, curated feel (Q14, Q15). His ideal body wash includes 'minimalist packaging with ingredient list' (Q49) — design matters but as a signal of substance, not status.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would respond positively to concepts where design signals product intelligence and thoughtfulness (clean typography, ingredient transparency on packaging) but negatively to concepts where design signals luxury or status. Would appreciate a concept that looks like it belongs on a Minimalist or Plum shelf — premium through restraint. Would not pay extra for design alone but design quality could be a tiebreaker.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage with ads that blend aesthetic quality with substantive content — beautifully shot ingredient breakdowns, well-designed infographics about formulation. Would tune out pure lifestyle/aspiration ads but also wouldn't engage with purely clinical/dry content. Influencer content works when the influencer has both good taste AND product knowledge. Would respond to 'smart choice' positioning over 'premium choice' positioning.

archetypes.dimension_name: Receptivity to Masculinity Framing in Marketing

selected_question.question_text: Two identical body washes: one labeled 'For Men' (₹400), another 'Unisex' (₹350). You're told the formulation is exactly the same. Which do you buy?
selected_question.why_selected: This question is a near-perfect isolator for masculinity-framing receptivity. By holding formulation constant and varying only the gendered label and a small price premium, it forces the respondent to reveal whether the 'For Men' tag adds perceived value, feels irrelevant, or actively deters them. For this specific person — who described wanting a 'masculine aftershave-like fragrance' (Q49) yet rates gender-neutral brands like Plum and Minimalist at 5/5 (Q48), values ingredients over branding (Q11), and has low brand loyalty (Q17) — the answer will disambiguate whether his masculine fragrance preference extends to gendered marketing framing or is purely a sensory preference. The ₹50 price difference also activates his price-consciousness (he'd look for alternatives at a 30% hike per Q26) and his stated willingness to pay only for clinical-grade differentiation (Q16), making the trade-off highly diagnostic.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd probably go for the 'For Men' one. Even if the formulation is the same, the fact that it's positioned for men makes me feel like it was designed with my skin and body odor concerns in mind. ₹50 extra isn't a big deal if it feels like it's made for me — like how I'd pick an aftershave-scented body wash over something generic. It just sits better in my bathroom.
archetypes.why_plausible: He explicitly described wanting a 'masculine aftershave-like fragrance' in his ideal body wash (Q49), rated The Man Company 4/5 (Q48), follows male actors and male beauty influencers (Q55), and values products that feel targeted to his specific concerns like oiliness and body odor in Indian heat (Q52). A preference for gendered framing as a relevance signal is consistent with these data points.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would respond positively to a body wash concept with dark, bold packaging, 'engineered for men's skin' positioning, and claims like 'controls oil and odor for the Indian man.' Would perceive such a product as more relevant and effective than a gender-neutral equivalent, and would be willing to pay a modest premium for the gendered positioning.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage strongly with ads featuring male-coded imagery — a confident guy getting ready for work, deep voiceover, gym or cricket references. A 'The Man Company'-style ad with masculine energy would feel aspirational and relatable. Gender-neutral or soft-toned ads would feel less personally relevant and less likely to prompt a click-through.

archetypes.answer_text: If the formulation is literally the same, I'd just buy the ₹350 unisex one — why pay more for a label? I don't mind 'For Men' branding, it doesn't bother me, but I'm not going to pay extra for it. I care about what's inside the bottle, not what's written on it. If the 'For Men' one was cheaper or had better packaging, sure, I'd pick that instead.
archetypes.why_plausible: He ranks ingredients and expert opinion far above packaging and brand (Q11), has low brand loyalty (Q17), would look for alternatives at a 30% price hike (Q26), and pays 0% premium for anything except clinical-grade formulation (Q16). His top-rated brands span gendered (The Man Company 4/5) and gender-neutral (Plum, Minimalist, Dot & Key all 5/5) without apparent pattern. This pragmatic, ingredient-first approach is highly consistent with his profile.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would evaluate a body wash concept purely on formulation claims, ingredient list, and price — regardless of whether it's positioned as 'for men' or unisex. A masculine-framed concept wouldn't deter him but also wouldn't add perceived value. Would respond equally to a gender-neutral clinical concept and a masculine clinical concept if the substance is identical.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage with ads based on content quality — ingredient breakdowns, dermatologist backing, aesthetic visuals — rather than gendered cues. A hyper-masculine ad wouldn't repel him but wouldn't outperform a well-made gender-neutral ad. The deciding factor would be whether the ad shows real product substance (ingredients, clinical data) vs. relying on vibes alone.

archetypes.answer_text: Definitely the unisex one. 'For Men' labeling on body wash feels like a marketing gimmick — it's the same product with a darker bottle and a higher price. Brands like Minimalist and Plum don't need to slap a gender on their packaging to be effective. I'd rather buy from a brand that respects my intelligence than one that thinks I need a 'manly' label to feel good about a body wash.
archetypes.why_plausible: He prefers small-scale, innovative brands over large corporations (Q14), rates Plum, Minimalist, and Dot & Key (all gender-neutral) at 5/5 while rating Beardo (heavily masculine-branded) at 2/5 (Q48). He values minimalist packaging (Q41), trusts ingredient lists over packaging claims (Q18 — packaging claims rated 1/5), and was disappointed by Bath & Body Works' marketing claims not matching reality (Q23). A skepticism toward gendered branding as performative marketing is well-supported.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would be actively turned off by a body wash concept with aggressive masculine positioning — dark packaging, 'built for men' tagline, and gendered claims. Would perceive it as overpriced and gimmicky. Would strongly prefer a gender-neutral concept with clean design, transparent ingredient list, and clinical backing. The same formulation in masculine vs. neutral packaging would see dramatically different purchase intent.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would scroll past or react negatively to hyper-masculine ads (gym scenes, 'man up your shower' taglines). Would find such ads try-hard and associate them with brands like Beardo that he rated poorly. Would engage much more with science-forward, aesthetically clean ads — think Minimalist-style content with ingredient breakdowns, or relatable everyday scenarios without gendered posturing. Influencer content would only work if the influencer feels authentic, not performing masculinity.

archetypes.dimension_name: Decision Urgency and Deliberation Window Length

selected_question.question_text: Zepto shows: 'Only 2 left' for a ₹400 body wash you've been considering. The regular price was ₹500, now discounted. Do you buy immediately or wait?
selected_question.why_selected: This question directly probes the temporal architecture of his decision-making by pitting urgency cues (scarcity + discount) against his established pattern of slow-burn evaluation for new products. The phrase 'you've been considering' mirrors his real Plum journey (Q58) where he was aware of a product but hadn't yet converted. His heavy Blinkit usage (Q25) means the quick-commerce context is natural, and the scarcity/discount framing tests whether urgency triggers override his multi-stage research process. This question uniquely separates impulsive converters from deliberate evaluators in a scenario that feels authentic to his actual shopping behavior.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd buy it immediately. If I've already been considering it and it's discounted with only 2 left, that's enough signal for me. I'm already on the app, the price is in my range, and I don't want to lose the deal. I can always return it or not repurchase if it doesn't work out — at ₹400 it's not a huge risk.
archetypes.why_plausible: He's rated 5/5 on comfort trying new brands (Q7), has low brand loyalty (Q17), and already uses Blinkit impulsively when products run out (Q25). His Bath & Body Works purchase (Q23) shows he's willing to buy on impulse in the right context. His budget of ₹350-500 (Q21) means ₹400 is within his normal range, lowering the perceived risk of a quick decision.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would give high purchase intent scores (8-9/10) on first exposure to a well-positioned concept. Would respond strongly to concepts with clear ingredient lists and clinical claims, simulating the decision as if buying now. Limited edition or introductory pricing cues would significantly boost his stated intent. His concept test scores would closely predict actual conversion.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: A single Instagram ad with a 'Shop Now on Blinkit' CTA would be highly effective — he'd tap through and potentially convert in one session. Scarcity messaging ('limited stock') and discount framing would accelerate action. Retargeting would be unnecessary; the first well-crafted ad impression could drive purchase. Pushy CTAs wouldn't bother him — they'd feel like helpful shortcuts.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd probably screenshot it and check the ingredients and reviews later. 'Only 2 left' doesn't really pressure me — these apps always restock. I've been considering it, sure, but I haven't done proper research yet. I'd rather check if a dermatologist or someone credible has reviewed it, look at the ingredient list properly, and then buy it next time it shows up on Blinkit or Nykaa.
archetypes.why_plausible: His Plum journey (Q58) is textbook slow-burn: social media awareness → seeing on Blinkit → further research → trial. He ranks expert opinion and ingredients above all other factors (Q11). He consulted ChatGPT before choosing his face cleanser (Q34). His trust hierarchy (Q18) shows he needs dermatologist-level validation, not just app availability. He's skeptical of packaging claims (trust rating: 1/5).
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would give moderate purchase intent scores (5-6/10) even for concepts he'd eventually buy. Responses would be hedged — 'I'd look into it' or 'interesting, would need to check ingredients.' His stated intent would significantly underpredict actual conversion because he needs multiple exposures and a situational trigger (running out). Urgency cues like limited editions would not move his scores.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: A single ad impression would register awareness but not drive action. He'd need a sustained multi-touchpoint campaign: Instagram awareness → ingredient breakdown content → influencer validation → seeing it on Blinkit when his current product runs out. A strong CTA like 'Order now' would feel pushy and reduce trust. Retargeting sequences over 2-3 weeks would be essential for conversion.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: Context-dependent (impulsive for replenishment, deliberate for new brands)
archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, it depends on where I am with my current body wash. If my Dettol is running low and I need something soon anyway, I'd just grab it — the discount makes it easy to justify trying something new. But if I still have product at home, I'd save it for later and do some research first. I don't feel urgency from 'only 2 left' — I feel urgency from needing to shower tomorrow morning.
archetypes.why_plausible: This directly reconciles the contradiction identified in the dimension analysis: his Blinkit behavior (Q25) is impulsive and need-triggered ('realizes product is empty right before bathing'), while his new brand evaluation (Q58, Plum) is multi-stage. His Bath & Body Works trial (Q23) was triggered by a situational context (being in the mall), not urgency. His decision mode genuinely shifts based on whether it's replenishment vs. exploration.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would give split responses depending on how the concept is framed. If positioned as a replacement for his current body wash with clear functional benefits (oil control, moisturizing), he'd score higher intent (7-8/10). If positioned as a novel/premium exploration, he'd score moderate (5-6/10) with caveats about needing to research. Concepts that combine 'everyday replacement' framing with clinical credibility would maximize his stated intent.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Effectiveness would depend entirely on timing and framing. An ad that catches him when his current product is running low (retargeted based on purchase cycle) with a Blinkit availability callout would convert quickly. The same ad at other times would only build awareness. Multi-touchpoint campaigns would be optimal: awareness ads for the slow-burn phase, then a conversion ad with availability/convenience messaging timed to his replenishment cycle. 'Available on Blinkit' would matter more than 'Buy now.'

archetypes.dimension_name: Skepticism Toward Novel Product Claims and Positioning

selected_question.question_text: A new D2C body wash brand claims: 'Clinically tested 2% niacinamide formula that controls oil for 12 hours, fades dark spots in 4 weeks, and replaces your post-shower moisturizer — all in one product.' The packaging looks clean and minimal, the price is ₹399 for 300ml, and it has 200 reviews averaging 4.3 stars on Nykaa. Walk me through your honest reaction — what do you believe, what do you doubt, and what would you need to see before buying?
selected_question.why_selected: This question is purpose-built to probe the exact 'prove it to me' threshold described in the dimension. It combines multiple elements this person has shown sensitivity to: multi-benefit claims (which he was burned by with Bath & Body Works 3-in-1), clinical/ingredient language he values (niacinamide, clinical testing), D2C brand positioning he's open to (Minimalist, Plum), dark spot targeting he explicitly wants (Q49), and oil control relevant to his skin concern (Q31). The multi-layered claim structure forces him to reveal whether he accepts, partially filters, or wholesale rejects stacked promises. No existing question in the bank directly probes skepticism toward novel claims — the closest (QN013, QN040) only touch adjacent territory without asking him to decompose a claim into believed vs. doubted components.

archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, this sounds like they crammed every trending claim into one product — oil control AND dark spots AND moisturizer replacement? I got burned by Bath & Body Works doing the 3-in-1 thing, so I'm not falling for that again. The niacinamide part I'd believe because Minimalist uses it and I've seen it work, but 12-hour oil control and 4-week dark spot results? I'd need to see the actual clinical study — not just 'clinically tested' written on the bottle. I'd probably search for dermatologist reviews or breakdowns on YouTube before even considering it.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with his negative Bath & Body Works 3-in-1 experience (Q23, Q51), his top ranking of expert opinion (Q11), his trust in dermatologists (Q18), his independent verification via ChatGPT (Q34), and his willingness to pay premium only for clinical-grade products (Q16). His frustration with unsubstantiated claims is documented.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would rate purchase intent low for any multi-benefit body wash concept unless the concept itself includes specific clinical evidence (study size, duration, dermatologist name/credentials). Would score believability low on stacked claims and flag them as 'marketing speak.' Would respond positively only to concepts that lead with one strong, substantiated claim rather than multiple unproven ones.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would actively resist aspirational ads making bold transformation claims without evidence. Would respond best to ads featuring named dermatologists explaining mechanism of action, or showing actual clinical trial data. Influencer testimonials alone would not convert him — he'd need the influencer to show ingredient breakdowns or reference studies. Would likely scroll past a glossy Instagram ad but stop for a 'science explainer' format.

archetypes.answer_text: The niacinamide part makes sense to me — I've used Minimalist products with it and they work. Oil control for 12 hours feels like a stretch but I'd give it a shot since that's my main skin concern anyway. The dark spots claim I'd take with a pinch of salt — 4 weeks is very specific and feels like marketing. Replacing moisturizer? Maybe. The price is reasonable and the packaging sounds like my type. I'd probably check the full ingredient list on Nykaa, skim a few reviews, and if nothing looks off, I'd add it to cart next time I'm on Blinkit.
archetypes.why_plausible: Aligns with his ingredient-first evaluation approach (Q11, Q24), his positive experience with Minimalist (Q48, Q34), his comfort trying new brands (Q7: 5/5), and his typical purchase flow of discovering online then buying on quick commerce (Q58, Q25). He does research but doesn't require exhaustive proof — he tried Plum after seeing it on social media and doing 'some reading' (Q58).
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would evaluate each claim independently rather than accepting or rejecting the whole concept. Would rate believability moderate-to-high for ingredient-backed claims (niacinamide, oil control) but lower for time-bound transformation claims (4-week dark spots). Would show higher purchase intent than Archetype A if the concept highlights recognized active ingredients, even without full clinical substantiation.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage with ads that lead with ingredient storytelling — showing what niacinamide does at a molecular level or comparing formulation to competitors. Would be moderately persuaded by influencer content if the influencer discusses ingredients rather than just results. Would not need full clinical data in the ad but would want enough specificity to feel the brand 'knows what they're doing.' Aesthetic, minimal ad design would hold his attention.

archetypes.answer_text: This actually sounds like it could be close to my ideal body wash — I literally said I want something that removes oil and targets dark spots with minimal packaging. ₹399 is in my range, 4.3 stars with 200 reviews is decent, and if they're using niacinamide at 2% that's a legit concentration. The 'replaces moisturizer' part is a bonus — if it works, that's one less product I need. I'd probably just order it on Blinkit next time my Dettol runs out and see for myself. Worst case, I switch back.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with his very high openness to new brands (Q7: 5/5), low brand loyalty (Q17), his ideal body wash description matching these claims almost exactly (Q49), and his pattern of trying products based on alignment with his stated needs without extensive pre-purchase verification (Q25 — impulse buying on quick commerce). He rated Plum, Minimalist, and Dot & Key all 5/5 (Q48), suggesting he may accept D2C brand claims more readily when the positioning resonates with his values.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would rate purchase intent high for multi-benefit concepts that align with his stated needs (oil control, dark spots, moisturization). Would take 'clinically tested' at face value without requiring the actual study. Would be excited by concepts that promise to simplify his routine (fewer products needed). Would rate believability higher across the board compared to other archetypes.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would respond enthusiastically to aspirational ads showing transformation results, aesthetic product shots, and lifestyle content. Influencer endorsements from relatable male creators would be sufficient to drive trial. Would not need clinical data in the ad — ingredient mentions (niacinamide, sulphate-free) serve as enough credibility signals. Quick commerce availability shown in the ad ('Available on Blinkit') would be a strong conversion trigger.

dimensions.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether this person processes advertising primarily through emotional resonance (mood, storytelling, aesthetic feeling) or through analytical evaluation (claims scrutiny, ingredient lists, comparative logic). The 59 questions capture WHAT content types they prefer (before/after, ingredient breakdowns) but not HOW they cognitively process an ad when first exposed — do they feel first and think later, or think first and feel later?
dimensions.concept_test_impact: An emotional processor would respond strongly to a concept that leads with a sensory narrative ('imagine stepping out of the shower feeling like silk on a rainy morning') while an analytical processor would respond better to a structured concept card listing pH level, ingredient sourcing, and clinical test results. Both could have identical answers to all 59 questions yet diverge sharply on concept appeal scores.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: When shown a 30-second video ad, an emotional processor would be swayed by music, color palette, and the protagonist's facial expressions, while an analytical processor would focus on the text overlays, claims, and product shots. This determines whether a mood-driven brand film or a fact-heavy explainer ad drives higher purchase intent.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: She describes herself as 'dramatic' and 'moody' (Q6), which hints at emotional processing, but she also ranks ingredients first (Q11) and prefers ingredient breakdowns (Q57), suggesting analytical tendencies. She could be a hybrid or lean either way — the 59 questions don't resolve this tension because they never measure real-time ad processing behavior.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (emotional processor) sees a body wash ad with a woman in a lavender field, soft music, and minimal text — she feels drawn in, imagines the scent, and rates purchase intent 8/10. Twin B (analytical processor) sees the same ad and feels it's 'vague,' wants to know the formulation details, and rates purchase intent 4/10. Same person profile, opposite reactions.

dimensions.dimension_name: Sensitivity to Aspirational vs Relatable Positioning
dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether this person responds more positively to aspirational brand positioning (luxury lifestyle, elevated self-image, premium aesthetics) or relatable positioning (someone like me, everyday authenticity, real-life scenarios). The 59 questions cover brand preferences and price sensitivity but never measure psychological response to positioning tone and social identity signaling in marketing.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: When shown two concepts — one positioned as 'indulgent spa luxury for the modern woman' with premium imagery vs one positioned as 'honest skincare for real, sensitive skin like yours' with everyday imagery — her preference would diverge dramatically based on this dimension. This affects willingness to pay, perceived product quality, and emotional connection to the brand.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: An aspirational-leaning twin would engage more with ads featuring polished aesthetics, upscale bathrooms, and glamorous models, while a relatable-leaning twin would engage more with ads showing a regular woman in a normal bathroom talking honestly about skin sensitivity. Click-through rates and brand recall would differ significantly.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: She uses Baylis & Harding (a somewhat premium international brand) suggesting some aspirational pull, but she also wants 'simple/cute packaging' (Q49) and spends only ₹100-200 (Q21), suggesting relatable groundedness. Her preference for clinical-looking packaging (Q42) adds further ambiguity — it could signal either sophistication-seeking or practicality. The tension is unresolved.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (aspirational) sees a concept for a lavender-mogra body wash positioned as 'luxury Ayurvedic ritual' with gold accents — she feels elevated and willing to pay ₹350. Twin B (relatable) sees the same concept and thinks it's 'trying too hard,' feels disconnected, and prefers a simpler version at ₹200. Both have identical 59-answer profiles.

dimensions.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Salience in Purchase Decisions
dimensions.what_it_captures: The degree to which visual design elements of packaging (color scheme, typography, bottle shape, shelf standout) influence this person's initial interest and final purchase decision, beyond the stated preference for 'clinical-looking' packaging. The 59 questions ask about packaging style preference but not how much packaging aesthetics actually drive behavior — whether she'd pick up a product based on visual appeal alone or largely ignores packaging if ingredients check out.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: When shown two identical formulations with different packaging designs — one with striking visual design and one with plain/generic design — a high visual-salience person would rate the attractive package significantly higher on purchase intent, perceived quality, and willingness to pay. A low visual-salience person would rate them nearly identically.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: In digital ads, a high visual-salience person would be more influenced by product photography, packaging close-ups, and unboxing aesthetics. A low visual-salience person would scroll past beautiful packaging shots but stop for ingredient callouts or testimonials. This directly affects which ad creative formats perform better.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: She wants 'simple/cute packaging' (Q49) and prefers 'clinical-looking' (Q42), giving us style preference but not salience weight. She ranks ingredients and reviews above brand (Q11), suggesting she may not be packaging-driven — but 'picky' and 'dramatic' (Q6) personality traits could mean aesthetics matter more than her stated priorities suggest. The actual behavioral weight of packaging remains unmeasured.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (high visual salience) sees a body wash concept mockup with a beautifully designed lavender bottle — the packaging alone makes her want to try it, and she rates purchase intent 9/10. Twin B (low visual salience) sees the same mockup, barely notices the design, flips to the ingredient list, and rates purchase intent 6/10 because she needs more information.

dimensions.dimension_name: Risk Framing Sensitivity in Product Claims
dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether this person responds more strongly to gain-framed messaging ('nourishes and protects your sensitive skin') or loss-framed messaging ('prevents irritation and damage to sensitive skin'). This is a well-established psychological dimension that shapes how product claims are evaluated, but none of the 59 questions measure whether she's motivated more by achieving positive outcomes or avoiding negative ones in the personal care context.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A gain-frame-sensitive person would rate a concept higher when it says 'leaves skin feeling soft and radiant' while a loss-frame-sensitive person would respond more to 'shields skin from harsh chemicals and irritation.' The same product benefits, framed differently, would produce meaningfully different purchase intent scores and perceived relevance.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Loss-frame-sensitive twins would respond more to ads showing skin problems being solved ('no more redness after every shower') while gain-frame-sensitive twins would respond to ads showing positive transformation ('discover your skin's natural glow'). This directly determines which ad copy and narrative arc drives higher engagement and conversion.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: Her switching triggers include 'skin problem' and 'doctor recommendation' (Q22), and she mentions hot water causing 'redness/bumps' (Q30) — suggesting some loss-aversion and problem-avoidance orientation. But she also describes ideal routines with positive aspirations like organic facemasks and juicing (Q10), suggesting gain motivation. The 59 questions reveal her concerns but not which framing psychologically moves her more in a purchase moment.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (loss-frame sensitive) sees an ad saying 'Stop exposing your sensitive skin to hidden irritants' — she feels urgency and clicks immediately. Twin B (gain-frame sensitive) sees the same ad and finds it fear-mongering; she'd respond better to 'Give your sensitive skin the gentle care it deserves.' Same profile, different click-through behavior.

dimensions.dimension_name: Contextual Mood State Receptivity to Marketing
dimensions.what_it_captures: How much this person's receptivity to product concepts and ads varies based on her current mood state and situational context (stressed vs relaxed, rushed vs leisurely, alone vs with others). She self-describes as 'moody' and her bathing routine is 'mood-dependent,' but the 59 questions never measure how mood fluctuation affects her openness to marketing messages, willingness to engage with new concepts, or susceptibility to impulse consideration.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A highly mood-dependent person might rate the same body wash concept 8/10 when relaxed on a weekend and 3/10 when stressed on a weekday — creating massive variance in concept test results depending on when they encounter it. A mood-stable person would give consistent ratings regardless of context. This affects how reliably concept scores predict real-world behavior.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: When shown an ad during a stressful workday scroll, a mood-volatile person might dismiss it entirely, but the same ad shown during a relaxed evening browse might trigger deep engagement and add-to-cart behavior. A mood-stable person would respond similarly in both contexts. This determines whether ad timing and placement strategy matters more than creative for this person.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: She explicitly says she's 'moody' (Q6) and her bathing is 'mood-dependent' (Q32), strongly suggesting mood plays a role in her personal care behavior. However, we don't know whether this moodiness extends to how she processes marketing stimuli — she could be moody about bathing but highly consistent in how she evaluates products and ads. The 59 questions capture mood's role in her routine but not in her marketing receptivity.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (high mood-context sensitivity) sees a calming lavender body wash ad while stressed — she's drawn to it as self-care escape and saves it for later purchase. Twin B (low mood-context sensitivity) sees the same ad while stressed — she evaluates it the same way she always would, checking ingredients and price, unaffected by her emotional state. Same profile, different engagement patterns and purchase timing.

archetypes.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style

selected_question.question_text: Imagine you're scrolling Instagram and you see two ads for a new lavender-based body wash for sensitive skin. Ad A is a 30-second video: a woman walks through a misty lavender garden at dawn, soft piano music plays, she closes her eyes in the shower and smiles — no text, no ingredient callouts, just a brand name at the end. Ad B is a carousel post: slide 1 shows the product, slide 2 lists pH level and key ingredients, slide 3 has a dermatologist's quote, slide 4 compares it to 3 competitors on gentleness. Which ad makes you more likely to tap 'Shop Now,' and what specifically about it pulls you in?
selected_question.why_selected: This question directly probes the emotional-vs-analytical processing tension that the 59 questions leave unresolved. It uses lavender (her stated favorite scent, Q36-37) and sensitive skin (her core concern, Q33) so both ads are equally relevant to her needs — the only differentiator is HOW she processes the information. Her profile contains contradictory signals: 'dramatic, moody' personality (Q6) and scent-as-internal-wellbeing (Q37) suggest emotional processing, while ingredients-first ranking (Q11), preference for ingredient breakdowns (Q57), and clinical-looking packaging preference (Q42) suggest analytical processing. This scenario forces her to reveal which mode dominates her real-time ad engagement behavior, with direct implications for concept and campaign testing.

archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, Ad A would stop my scroll immediately. The lavender garden, the soft music, that moment of her closing her eyes in the shower — I could literally feel the scent and the calm. That's exactly what I want from my body wash, that feel-good vibe. I'd tap Shop Now on that mood alone and then maybe check ingredients on the product page later, but the feeling has to hit first.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with her self-description as 'dramatic' and 'moody' (Q6), her emphasis on scent as internal well-being rather than external perception (Q8, Q37), and her love of lavender specifically for its 'refreshing, natural feel-good vibe' (Q37). Her ideal body wash is described in sensory-emotional terms (Q49). She also mentioned grooming is mood-dependent (Q32), suggesting emotional states drive her engagement.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would rate a body wash concept significantly higher if it leads with a sensory narrative ('imagine the calm of fresh lavender on your skin after a long day') over a structured ingredient-benefit table. Would respond strongly to concepts with evocative imagery, floral mood boards, and experiential language. Would undervalue concepts that lead with pH levels or clinical data even if the formulation is identical.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would show high purchase intent for mood-driven brand films with music, soft visuals, and minimal text. Would engage longer with video ads that create an atmospheric experience. Would scroll past fact-heavy carousel ads or explainer videos. Influencer content that shows the 'ritual' and 'feeling' of using the product would outperform ingredient-review content.

archetypes.answer_text: Ad B, no question. Ad A is pretty but tells me nothing — I have sensitive skin and I can't just trust vibes. I need to see the pH level, what's actually in it, and whether a dermatologist backs it. The comparison with competitors is exactly what I'd do myself anyway. Beautiful ads are nice but they don't tell me if it'll break my skin out.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with her ranking ingredients as the #1 purchase factor (Q11), preference for ingredient breakdowns and before/after content (Q57), distrust of packaging claims (Q17: 1/5), preference for clinical-looking packaging (Q42), reliance on dermatologist recommendations (Q17: 4/5), and her stated frustration with products that promise but underdeliver (Q53). She researches extensively on YouTube, Amazon, Google (Q58) and would never try something on a friend's recommendation alone (Q59: 1/5).
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would rate concepts higher when they lead with specific ingredient callouts, dermatologist endorsements, and sensitive-skin clinical data. Would be skeptical of concepts using purely evocative or aspirational language. Would want to see formulation details, sourcing transparency, and comparative claims before rating purchase intent.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would show higher purchase intent for carousel/explainer ads with ingredient lists, dermatologist quotes, and competitive comparisons. Would dismiss atmospheric brand films as 'vague' or 'all marketing.' Would engage most with YouTube long-form reviews that break down formulations. Text overlays and data points in video ads would increase her attention and trust.

archetypes.position_on_dimension: context-dependent hybrid — emotional hook triggers analytical deep-dive
archetypes.answer_text: Ad A would catch my eye for sure — the lavender garden and that shower moment would make me feel something. But I wouldn't tap Shop Now from that alone. I'd probably screenshot it, then go search for the brand on Amazon or YouTube to check ingredients and reviews. So Ad A gets my attention, but Ad B's information is what I'd actually need before buying. I need both, honestly.
archetypes.why_plausible: This resolves the tension in her profile: she's 'dramatic and moody' (Q6) with strong sensory preferences (lavender, mogra, scent-as-wellbeing in Q36-37) but also deeply research-driven (Q58: YouTube, Amazon, Google), ingredients-first (Q11), and extremely cautious about new products (Q7: 1/5 comfort with new brands, Q59: 1/5 trust in friend recs). Her discovery channels are visual/social (YouTube, Instagram, Q54) but her conversion channels are analytical (reviews, ingredients). This two-stage processing is consistent with someone who is emotionally drawn in but analytically closes.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would respond best to concepts that layer emotional storytelling on top of substantive ingredient/clinical information — e.g., a concept card that opens with a sensory narrative but includes a clear ingredient panel and dermatologist endorsement below. Would rate purely emotional OR purely analytical concepts as incomplete. Would show moderate-to-high intent only when both elements are present.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage initially with atmospheric video ads (longer view time, saves/screenshots) but would not convert directly from them — would need a follow-up touchpoint with detailed information. A campaign strategy pairing a mood-driven brand film (awareness) with a retargeting carousel of ingredients/reviews (conversion) would maximize her purchase intent. Single-format campaigns of either type alone would underperform.

archetypes.dimension_name: Sensitivity to Aspirational vs Relatable Positioning

selected_question.question_text: Two body washes have identical reviews and ingredients. One costs ₹300 with plain packaging, the other ₹450 with Instagram-worthy design. Which influences your purchase decision more?
selected_question.why_selected: This question directly isolates the aspirational vs relatable tension in this person's profile. She values ingredients above all (Q11, Q24) and wants 'simple/cute packaging' (Q49), yet she also chooses Baylis & Harding for its premium international appeal (Q14) and prefers clinical-looking packaging (Q42). By holding ingredients and reviews constant, this question forces a pure positioning/aesthetics trade-off — does she pay more for elevated visual identity (aspirational) or stick with substance over style (relatable/practical)? Her ₹100-200 typical spend (Q21) and price sensitivity add real tension to the ₹150 premium. This is the cleanest probe for whether packaging-as-identity-signal matters to her beyond functional claims.

archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, if the ingredients are the same, I'd go for the ₹450 one with the better packaging. I like my bathroom shelf to look put-together — it's a small luxury that makes me feel good. I already spend on Baylis & Harding partly because it looks and feels premium, so ₹150 extra for something that gives me that elevated vibe is worth it. Presentation matters to me even if I won't admit it easily.
archetypes.why_plausible: She already buys Baylis & Harding, an international premium brand (Q14), and prefers clinical-looking packaging (Q42) which signals sophistication. She describes herself as 'dramatic' and 'picky' (Q6), and her ideal body wash at ₹250 (Q49) shows she's willing to stretch beyond her ₹100-200 usual spend for the right product. Her loyalty to specific brands (Q16) suggests brand identity matters to her beyond pure function.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would respond positively to a lavender-mogra body wash positioned as 'luxury Ayurvedic ritual' with elegant packaging and gold accents. Would perceive higher product quality from premium visual cues and be willing to pay ₹300-350. Would rate concepts with minimalist-premium aesthetics higher on purchase intent even at a price premium.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage more with ads featuring polished bathroom settings, soft lighting, and an elevated self-care narrative. Instagram-style aesthetics with curated visuals would drive higher click-through and brand recall. Influencer content from aspirational lifestyle creators would resonate more than testimonial-style honest reviews.

archetypes.answer_text: ₹300 plain packaging, no question. If the ingredients and reviews are the same, why would I pay ₹150 more for a pretty bottle? That's just marketing fluff. I'd rather save that money or put it toward something that actually benefits my sensitive skin. I care about what's inside, not what it looks like on Instagram.
archetypes.why_plausible: She ranks ingredients first in purchase decisions (Q11, Q24), rates packaging claims lowest in trust (Q17), and her typical spend is ₹100-200 (Q21). She was disappointed by a ₹350 purchase that 'underdelivered' (Q51-53), showing price-value sensitivity. Her preference for 'simple/cute packaging' (Q49) and focus on skin sensitivity (Q33) suggest function-first thinking. She doesn't follow beauty influencers (Q55 skipped) and her only social media purchase was 'mediocre' (Q56).
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would prefer a body wash concept positioned as 'honest skincare for real sensitive skin' with straightforward claims and simple packaging. Would be skeptical of luxury positioning, viewing premium aesthetics as a red flag for overpricing. Would rate a ₹200 relatable concept higher on purchase intent than a ₹350 aspirational one with identical formulation.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage more with ads showing a regular person in a normal bathroom honestly discussing sensitive skin struggles. Before/after content (Q57) and ingredient breakdowns would drive engagement. Would scroll past polished luxury ads as 'trying too hard.' YouTube reviews and friend recommendations would outperform aspirational influencer content.

archetypes.answer_text: It depends on my mood honestly. If the plain one looks clinical and clean, I'd probably go with that — I like that aesthetic anyway. But if the ₹450 one has that international, sophisticated feel like my Baylis & Harding bottles, I might splurge occasionally. I wouldn't buy the fancy one every time, but as a treat when I'm feeling dramatic? Maybe. I'd still feel slightly guilty about the ₹150 difference though.
archetypes.why_plausible: She describes herself as 'moody' and 'dramatic' (Q6), and her grooming is 'mood-dependent' (Q32) — suggesting her positioning sensitivity shifts with emotional state. She oscillates between premium international brands (Baylis & Harding) and budget options (Pears, Lux) in her actual usage (Q43). She wants clinical-looking packaging (Q42) but also 'simple/cute' (Q49), showing she values understated sophistication rather than overt luxury. Her ambivert nature (Q6) mirrors this both-and positioning.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: Would respond to concepts that blend aspirational quality cues with relatable honesty — e.g., 'internationally inspired Ayurvedic care for sensitive skin' with clean, clinical-premium packaging. Would reject overtly luxurious concepts as disconnected but also find purely basic concepts uninspiring. Sweet spot is ₹250-300 with subtle premium signals.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: Would engage with ads that feel 'elevated but real' — a well-shot but not overly glamorous setting, someone relatable but put-together discussing genuine skin concerns. Would respond to content that balances ingredient education (Q57) with aesthetic appeal. Neither pure luxury nor pure testimonial ads would maximize engagement; hybrid tones would perform best.

archetypes.dimension_name: Visual Packaging Salience in Purchase Decisions

selected_question.question_text: Two body washes have identical reviews and ingredients. One costs ₹300 with plain packaging, the other ₹450 with Instagram-worthy design. Which influences your purchase decision more?
selected_question.why_selected: This question is a near-perfect direct probe for visual packaging salience because it isolates packaging aesthetics as the only variable while holding ingredients and reviews constant — the two factors this person ranks highest (Q11, Q24). It forces her to reveal the actual behavioral weight she gives to packaging design when her stated top priorities are already satisfied. The price differential (₹150 premium) is within her stated willingness-to-pay range (Q21, Q49), making both options financially plausible. Given her 'picky' and 'dramatic' personality (Q6), preference for 'simple/cute packaging' (Q49), and 'clinical-looking' style (Q42), this question directly tests whether those aesthetic preferences translate into actual purchase behavior or are secondary to functional evaluation.

archetypes.answer_text: Honestly, if the ingredients and reviews are the same, I'm picking the ₹300 one without thinking twice. I don't care how pretty the bottle looks — it's going to sit in my bathroom, not on my Instagram. That ₹150 saved can go towards a better face moisturizer or something that actually matters for my sensitive skin.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with her ranking ingredients and reviews as top purchase drivers (Q11, Q24), her strong loyalty to functional performance over aesthetics (Q16, Q44), her preference for 'clinical-looking' packaging (Q42) which suggests function-over-form orientation, and her practical approach of going to a physical store rather than settling for a random substitute (Q26). Her stated budget of ₹100-200 (Q21) also suggests price sensitivity when functional value is equal.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: When shown two identical body wash formulations with different packaging — one with a beautifully designed lavender bottle and one with plain clinical packaging — this twin would rate both nearly identically on purchase intent (perhaps 6-7/10 for both), focusing entirely on the ingredient list and sensitive-skin claims. She might even slightly prefer the plain clinical one as it signals 'serious' formulation.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: In digital ads, this twin would scroll past packaging beauty shots and unboxing content without engagement. She would stop for ingredient breakdowns, dermatologist endorsements, and before/after sensitive-skin testimonials. Ad creatives featuring close-ups of the ingredient panel or a 'what's inside' explainer would outperform lifestyle/aesthetic packaging shots by a wide margin.

archetypes.answer_text: I'd notice the pretty one for sure — I'm picky and I like things that look cute and clean. But ₹450 vs ₹300 when they're literally the same inside? I'd probably pick the ₹300 one but feel a little annoyed about it. If the price difference was smaller, like ₹50, I'd grab the nicer-looking one because why not — it makes the bathroom feel better.
archetypes.why_plausible: Aligns with her self-described 'picky' and 'dramatic' personality (Q6) that would naturally notice and appreciate aesthetics, while her stated desire for 'simple/cute packaging' (Q49) shows she does have packaging preferences. However, her ingredient-first ranking (Q11), budget consciousness (Q21: ₹100-200 range), and practical behavior of repurchasing the same product (Q19) suggest she wouldn't pay a steep premium purely for looks. The tension between her aesthetic sensibility and functional pragmatism is very real for this person.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: When shown two identical formulations with different packaging, this twin would rate the attractive package moderately higher (maybe 7/10 vs 6/10) on initial appeal and perceived quality, but the gap would narrow once she reads the ingredient list and confirms they're the same. She'd express a preference for the nicer design but wouldn't commit to paying significantly more for it.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: This twin would pause briefly on visually appealing packaging shots in ads — enough to register interest — but would need the ad to quickly transition to ingredient callouts or sensitive-skin claims to convert. A hybrid ad format combining beautiful product photography with an ingredient overlay or 'what's inside' text would perform best. Pure aesthetic ads would generate awareness but not purchase intent.

archetypes.answer_text: The Instagram-worthy one, 100%. I know it sounds dramatic but if something looks beautiful and well-designed, it just feels more premium and trustworthy to me. My Baylis & Harding bottles look gorgeous in my bathroom and that's part of why I love them. ₹450 is still within my range and if the packaging makes me happy every time I use it, that's worth it — it's like a little mood boost for my moody self.
archetypes.why_plausible: Consistent with her preference for international brands like Baylis & Harding (Q14) which are known for their elegant packaging design, her 'dramatic' and 'picky' personality (Q6), and her desire for 'simple/cute packaging' (Q49) which shows packaging IS part of her ideal product vision. Her most expensive purchase was ~₹350 (Q51), and ₹450 isn't far off. Her mood-dependent relationship with bathing (Q32) and emphasis on scent for 'internal well-being' (Q37) suggest she values the emotional/sensory experience of products — packaging could easily be part of that holistic experience.
archetypes.concept_test_prediction: When shown two identical formulations with different packaging, this twin would rate the beautifully designed one significantly higher on purchase intent (8-9/10 vs 5-6/10), perceived quality, and willingness to pay. She would associate the attractive packaging with gentleness and premium quality, projecting positive attributes onto the formulation based on visual cues alone.
archetypes.ad_test_prediction: This twin would be highly responsive to product photography, unboxing aesthetics, and lifestyle shots showing the bottle in a beautiful bathroom setting. She would engage with Instagram carousel ads featuring packaging close-ups and 'shelfie' content. Influencer content showing the product as part of an aesthetic routine would drive strong purchase intent, even before ingredient details are mentioned.

archetypes.dimension_name: Risk Framing Sensitivity in Product Claims

selected_question.question_text: You're browsing online and see two body washes, both Ayurvedic and priced at ₹250, designed for sensitive skin. Brand A's packaging says: 'Nourishes and restores your skin's natural softness with pure lavender and mogra extracts.' Brand B's packaging says: 'Protects sensitive skin from irritation, redness, and chemical damage with pure lavender and mogra extracts.' Same ingredients, same price, same scent. Which one do you instinctively reach for, and why?
selected_question.why_selected: None of the 291 bank questions directly probe gain-vs-loss framing sensitivity. This generated question is perfectly tailored to this person: it holds constant every factor she cares about (Ayurvedic, sensitive skin, lavender-mogra scent, ₹250 price point, ingredients) and isolates ONLY the framing variable. By matching her exact ideal product (Q49) and her key concerns (sensitivity, natural, floral scent), it removes confounds and forces a pure framing preference reveal. The two options mirror the exact dimension: gain-frame ('nourishes and restores') vs loss-frame ('protects from irritation and damage'). Her answer directly predicts how she'd respond to differently-framed product concepts and ad copy.

archetypes.answer_text: Brand B, hands down. My skin is super sensitive and I've dealt with enough redness and bumps from harsh products — I need something that explicitly promises to protect me from that. 'Nourishes and restores' sounds nice but vague. When a product says it protects from irritation and chemical damage, that's speaking my language. I've switched products before because of skin problems, so I want to know a product is actively shielding me.
